meta.description: Municipality-level physical climate risk scores for the Netherlands (indicative, for demonstration)
meta.exposure_formula: exposure_index = (0.55 × woz_norm + 0.45 × climate_composite) × 100  |  climate_composite = 0.50 × flood + 0.35 × foundation + 0.15 × drought
meta.flood_source: AHN4 DTM 0.5m (PDOK WCS) — median NAP elevation at municipality centroid
meta.heat_formula: heat_risk: urban density × UHI severity (province baseline + city overrides)
meta.pluvial_formula: pluvial_risk: impervious surface × drainage capacity × terrain slope proxy
meta.foundation_source: AHN4 NAP elevation (proxy: below-NAP = peat/clay polder) + province soil modifier (Randstad peat vs. Drenthe/Limburg sand)
meta.mortgage_source: CBS Woononderzoek 2022: owner-occupied (%) × 0.68 (fraction with bank mortgage)

GM0505.note: Dordrecht — island municipality in Rhine–Meuse delta; highest static flood exposure in NL without active pumping

GM0034.note: Almere — built entirely on reclaimed IJsselmeer polder; all land below sea level

GM0599.note: Rotterdam — largest port; much of city below sea level; continuous active flood management essential

GM0687.note: Middelburg — Zeeland delta; within the 1953 flood area; protected by Delta Works

GM0994.note: Valkenburg aan de Geul — severely flooded in 2021 Meuse disaster; narrow valley flood path

GM1931.note: Krimpenerwaard — situated between Lek and Hollandse IJssel rivers; deep peat polders

GM1901.note: Bodegraven-Reeuwijk — among the deepest peat soils in the Netherlands; severe ongoing subsidence

GM0513.note: Gouda — chronic peat subsidence; historic city centre foundations under long-term stress

GM0014.note: Groningen — gas extraction-induced seismicity compounds clay soil foundation risk; unique hazard profile

GM0363.note: Amsterdam — extensive canal network; peat subsoil beneath historic centre; 17th-century pile foundations ageing

GM0632.note: Woerden — Groene Hart peat subsidence zone; among highest foundation-damage claim rates in NL

GM0542.note: Krimpen aan den IJssel — river confluence; low-lying polder with high dike dependency

GM0479.note: Zaanstad — below sea level peat polders; industrial heritage on wooden pile foundations

GM0361.note: Alkmaar — historic polder city; below sea level; West Frisian canal network

GM0392.note: Haarlem — dune-backed but low hinterland; peat-on-clay subsoil common

GM0546.note: Leiden — at Rhine–North Sea connection; partly below sea level; older building stock

GM0518.note: Den Haag — coastal; Hofvijver area on peat; mixed risk profile

GM0344.note: Utrecht — Groene Hart edge; city centre on peat with documented foundation damage

GM0268.note: Nijmegen — Rhine/Waal floodplain; Room for the River intervention site

GM0772.note: Eindhoven — inland on sandy Pleistocene soil; drought stress for agri-horticulture sector

GM0637.note: Maastricht — Meuse flooding; 2021 extreme event; higher elevation limits subsidence

GM0080.note: Amersfoort — Utrecht peat fringe; moderate flood but foundation risk elevated

GM0106.note: Leiden — Rhine–North Sea junction; partly below sea level; peat/clay subsoil

GM0503.note: Delft — between Schie and Rhine canal; moderate flood; mostly on firmer soil

GM0888.note: Zwolle — IJssel floodplain; climate adaptation frontrunner municipality

GM0677.note: Leeuwarden — Frisian coastal plain; low-lying; managed water levels
